neighborhoods.name: Paseo Boricua / Division Street Corridor
primary_groups: Puerto Ricans displaced from Lincoln Park and Old Town by urban renewal
neighborhoods.character: The cultural heart of Chicago's Puerto Rican community, centered on Division Street between Western and California avenues. Two massive steel Puerto Rican flags arch over Division Street at either end of the corridor, installed in 1995 to mark the Paseo Boricua. Bodegas, restaurants, cultural organizations, and murals line the street. The corridor emerged after Puerto Rican families were displaced from Lincoln Park and Old Town during 1960s urban renewal and clustered in Humboldt Park, transforming what had been a Scandinavian and Polish working-class neighborhood.
key_institutions: Juan Antonio Corretjer Puerto Rican Cultural Center
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: When {ancestor_name} settled near Division Street in the {decade}s, the Paseo Boricua was becoming something rare in mainland cities — a stretch of blocks where Puerto Rican culture was not just tolerated but inscribed into the architecture itself, from the steel flags overhead to the casitas in the community gardens.

neighborhoods.name: West Humboldt Park
primary_groups: Puerto Ricans, African Americans, Mexican Americans
neighborhoods.character: West of the park itself, this section was more racially mixed than the Division Street corridor. Puerto Rican and African American families lived side by side in two-flats and three-flats. The area was more affordable but also more disinvested, with higher vacancy rates and fewer commercial services than the Paseo Boricua. Gang violence was a recurring challenge from the 1970s onward.
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: If {ancestor_name} lived on the west side of the park, the neighborhood was rougher around the edges than Division Street — but the two-flats were affordable, and a family could put down roots within walking distance of the park's green space.

neighborhoods.name: Lincoln Park / Old Town (pre-displacement)
primary_groups: Puerto Rican contract laborers, early migrants from island
neighborhoods.character: The original Puerto Rican settlement area in Chicago. Puerto Ricans began arriving after World War II, recruited for foundry work, domestic service, and light manufacturing. They settled in the affordable sections of Lincoln Park and Old Town, near North Avenue and around LaSalle Street. Urban renewal beginning in the late 1950s systematically demolished affordable housing, pushing Puerto Rican families westward into Humboldt Park and Logan Square.
key_institutions: Caballeros de San Juan (Knights of St. John)
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: The {surname} family's Chicago story may have begun in Lincoln Park before moving to Humboldt Park — thousands of Puerto Rican families were pushed west when urban renewal bulldozed the affordable flats around North Avenue in the early 1960s.

major_employers.name: Light Manufacturing (Metalwork and Food Processing)
major_employers.wages: $1.25-2.00/hr (1960s), $3.00-5.00/hr (1970s) — generally at or slightly above minimum wage
major_employers.working_conditions: Small and mid-size factories scattered through the industrial corridors of the Near West Side and along the Chicago River. Work was repetitive, often hot, and rarely unionized in the smaller shops. Many operations closed or relocated starting in the 1970s as deindustrialization hit the city.
major_employers.narrative_hook: If {ancestor_name} found factory work in the {decade}s, it was likely in one of the metalworking or food processing plants that employed Puerto Rican workers at close to minimum wage — steady enough to pay rent on a two-flat, but not enough to build much beyond that.

major_employers.name: Service Sector (Hotels, Restaurants, Hospitals)
major_employers.wages: $1.00-1.50/hr (1960s), $2.50-4.00/hr (1970s) — often minimum wage or below for tipped positions
major_employers.working_conditions: Many Humboldt Park residents commuted to the Loop and Near North Side for service jobs in hotels, restaurants, and hospitals. Hours were irregular, benefits rare, and language barriers limited advancement. Women were heavily represented in domestic and institutional cleaning work.
major_employers.narrative_hook: {ancestor_name} may have commuted downtown for service work — Humboldt Park was a bedroom community for the Loop's hotels, restaurants, and hospitals, and the Division Street bus was packed with workers heading east before dawn.

major_employers.name: Construction Industry
major_employers.wages: $2.00-4.00/hr (1960s), higher for union positions; day labor paid less
major_employers.working_conditions: Construction offered relatively high wages for manual labor but was seasonal and physically punishing. Puerto Rican workers faced discrimination from trade unions that controlled access to higher-paying skilled positions. Day labor and non-union subcontracting were common entry points.
major_employers.narrative_hook: Construction work paid better than the factories, but if {ancestor_name} worked the trades, breaking into a union hall in the {decade}s meant navigating ethnic gatekeeping that kept Puerto Rican workers in the lowest-paid positions.

housing_patterns.initial_settlement: Puerto Rican families first settled in Lincoln Park and Old Town in the late 1940s and 1950s, occupying affordable flats and rooming houses. After urban renewal displacement in the early 1960s, families moved west into Humboldt Park's existing housing stock of two-flats and three-flats, often doubling up with relatives while searching for permanent housing.
housing_patterns.ethnic_clustering: Puerto Rican settlement concentrated along Division Street and in the blocks surrounding Humboldt Park itself. The community was hemmed in by other ethnic enclaves — Polish and Ukrainian to the north, African American to the west, and gentrifying areas to the east. Casitas — small wooden structures modeled on rural Puerto Rican houses — appeared in vacant lots and community gardens, creating distinctly Puerto Rican spaces.
housing_patterns.trajectory: Shared flat with extended family (initial months) to rented two-flat apartment (1-5 years) to ownership of a two-flat or three-flat (first generation goal, often achieved by the 1970s-1980s). Starting in the 2000s, gentrification pressure from Wicker Park and Bucktown threatened to displace long-term Puerto Rican residents, with property taxes and rents rising sharply.

anti_immigrant_reception.description: Puerto Ricans are US citizens by birth, but that legal status did not prevent discrimination. Chicago's Puerto Rican community faced housing discrimination, police harassment, and displacement through urban renewal. The 1966 Division Street Riots — the first major Puerto Rican urban uprising in the United States — were sparked by the police shooting of Arcelis Cruz, a young Puerto Rican man, during the city's first Puerto Rican Day parade. Three days of unrest followed, and the aftermath catalyzed Puerto Rican political organizing in the city.
specific_incidents: 1966 Division Street Riots — police shooting of Arcelis Cruz sparked three days of civil unrest
1960s urban renewal displacement from Lincoln Park and Old Town
2000s gentrification displacement — rising rents and property taxes pushed long-term residents out
nativist_organizations: Lincoln Park Conservation Association (pushed urban renewal that displaced Puerto Ricans, 1960s)

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: Juan Antonio Corretjer Puerto Rican Cultural Center
churches_and_mutual_aid.community_served: Puerto Rican community of Humboldt Park
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Community event records, oral history collections, cultural programming archives. Not a genealogical records repository but documents community presence and participation.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: The Puerto Rican Cultural Center on Division Street has been the institutional anchor of Humboldt Park's Puerto Rican identity since 1973 — if {ancestor_name} was active in the community, their name may appear in event programs, organizing records, or oral histories collected there.

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: Caballeros de San Juan
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Membership records, community service records. Organized through Catholic parishes, originally in Lincoln Park, later in Humboldt Park parishes.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: The Caballeros de San Juan — the Knights of St. John — was the first major Puerto Rican community organization in Chicago. If {ancestor_name} was a Catholic man involved in community life in the 1950s or 1960s, the Caballeros were likely part of that story.

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: St. Sylvester Church
churches_and_mutual_aid.community_served: Puerto Rican and Latino Catholics in Humboldt Park
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Baptism, marriage, and death records in parish registers. Spanish-language masses began in the 1960s as the parish transitioned from its original Polish and Scandinavian congregations. Records held by Archdiocese of Chicago Archives.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: St. Sylvester's transition from a Polish parish to a Puerto Rican one mirrors the neighborhood's own transformation — if {ancestor_name} married or baptized children in Humboldt Park, the parish registers may hold those records.

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: First Congregational Church of Humboldt Park / Iglesia del Barrio
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Congregation records, community outreach documentation. Protestant churches served a significant minority of Puerto Rican families, particularly Pentecostal and evangelical congregations.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: Not every Puerto Rican family in Humboldt Park was Catholic — if {ancestor_name} attended one of the storefront Pentecostal churches or the Iglesia del Barrio, those congregations kept their own records of membership and baptism.

source_references.author: Gina M. Perez
source_references.title: The Near Northwest Side Story: Migration, Displacement, and Puerto Rican Families
source_references.notes: Ethnographic study of Puerto Rican families in Humboldt Park, documenting the effects of displacement, gentrification, and community organizing from the 1960s through the early 2000s.

source_references.author: Merida M. Rua
source_references.title: A Grounded Identidad: Making New Lives in Chicago's Puerto Rican Neighborhoods
source_references.notes: Examines the formation of Puerto Rican identity and community in Chicago's near northwest side neighborhoods, including interethnic relations with Mexican Americans.

source_references.author: Felix M. Padilla
source_references.title: Puerto Rican Chicago
source_references.notes: One of the earliest academic studies of Chicago's Puerto Rican community, covering migration patterns, labor market experiences, and community formation from the late 1940s through the 1980s.

source_references.author: Ana Y. Ramos-Zayas
source_references.title: National Performances: The Politics of Class, Race, and Space in Puerto Rican Chicago
source_references.notes: Examines political activism, identity formation, and the intersection of race and class in Humboldt Park's Puerto Rican community, with attention to the Paseo Boricua cultural district.

source_references.author: Lilia Fernandez
source_references.title: Brown in the Windy City: Mexicans and Puerto Ricans in Postwar Chicago
source_references.notes: Comparative study of Latino community formation in postwar Chicago, documenting how urban renewal, deindustrialization, and racial politics shaped both Mexican and Puerto Rican neighborhoods.
